source_title: INTERVIEW: Israel will achieve its Greater Israel project

interactions.summary: The host frames Jiang as a now-viral forecaster and asks for his immediate reading of the Middle East war now underway.
interactions.speaker_attribution: George Galloway host introduction to Jiang question

interactions.summary: Jiang predicts an eventual failed American ground invasion of Iran, a resulting collapse of American imperial position in the region, and an Israeli move toward a Greater Israel order culminating in Pax Judaica and the destruction of Al-Aqsa.
The host brackets the apocalyptic rhetoric and asks what a specifically nuclear Israeli strike on Iran would mean in practice.

Jiang says a tactical nuclear strike is part of Israeli-American planning discourse but argues it is unlikely because Israel's doctrine is oriented around all-out strategic annihilation rather than flexible tactical use.

Continuing his answer on nuclear use, Jiang argues that Israeli eschatology actually needs America to lose in Iran, so a limited ground-war trap is more plausible than a tactical nuclear strike, and he adds Russia as an escalation constraint.
The host asks what would happen if the United States tried the non-nuclear option of dropping troops into a huge, mountainous, now-rallied Iran.

Jiang says a parachute insertion would be suicidal, argues the June twelve-day war and later protest crackdowns unified Iran while burning Israeli networks, and says a real invasion would require proxies and mass troop staging that America does not presently control.
The host asks Jiang to estimate how much damage Iran is inflicting on Israel after noting censorship, missile strikes, and the possibility of Pakistani sympathy for Iran.

Jiang says Israel learned to censor visible destruction, argues the deeper vulnerability lies in GCC states that host American war capacity, and describes Iranian and possible false-flag strikes as part of a struggle over whether Gulf monarchies get dragged into the war.
The host escalates from battlefield damage to the oil-war scenario, asking whether mutual attacks on energy infrastructure imply an uncontrolled strike on Gulf oil and a total breakdown of pricing.

Jiang answers that the war has moved up the escalation ladder from military targets to economic soft targets, making cheap energy and the old global-trade order effectively finished and forcing de-industrialization and mercantilist supply-chain relocalization.
The host asks whether the attack on Iran is really a prelude to a larger U.S.-China showdown, given China's ties to Iran and dependence on global industrial trade.

Jiang rejects the immediate war-with-China frame and instead predicts a rapid U.S.-China rapprochement built on shared dependence within globalization, possibly extending to Chinese purchases of American energy.
The host returns to the Middle East and asks whether any off-ramp exists that could stop the war before it becomes total catastrophe.

Jiang says the United States structured negotiations around impossible demands, used failed diplomacy as pretext, and is keeping its strategy vague because the real goal is not a deal but the destruction and partition of Iran.
In his final coda, Jiang allows that mediation may be attempted by GCC states or China but says the underlying American drive remains the destruction of Iran.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is the interviewer introducing Jiang and asking the nuclear-strike follow-up.
SPEAKER_00 is Jiang delivering the opening geopolitical forecast and the nuclear-doctrine argument.
SPEAKER_01 is the host posing the ground-invasion hypothetical after Jiang's nuclear discussion.
SPEAKER_00 remains Jiang throughout the extended answer and the follow-up on invasion logistics.
SPEAKER_01 is the host in both the battlefield-damage question and the later oil-war escalation prompt.
SPEAKER_00 is Jiang in the damage assessment, Gulf-war analysis, and world-economy forecast.
SPEAKER_01 is the host in both the China question and the final off-ramp question.
SPEAKER_00 is Jiang throughout the China rapprochement argument and the closing war-purpose diagnosis.
SPEAKER_00 is Jiang closing the interview with a narrow concession to mediation before returning to his core diagnosis.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts that the United States will eventually launch a ground invasion of Iran and that the invasion will fail.
claims.temporal_scope: Prediction made in the interview dated 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that a failed American invasion of Iran will force the United States to retreat from the Middle East and leave CENTRAL COMMAND effectively absorbed into Israeli regional power.
Jiang says Israel aims at a Greater Israel project stretching from the Nile to the Euphrates and extending across neighboring Arab states and parts of Turkey.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's description of Israeli ideological geography in this interview.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts that regardless of how the Iran war ends, Israel will keep escalating against regional states, with Turkey becoming a later target after Iran.
Jiang defines Pax Judaica as a Jerusalem-based trading, financial, and technological empire presented as biblical fulfillment.
Jiang predicts that Al-Aqsa Mosque will eventually be destroyed and cites rumors and inflammatory rabbinic rhetoric as evidence of how such a destruction might be staged and justified.
claims.temporal_scope: Prediction about the next four to five years made on 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang says a tactical nuclear strike is discussed as a way to seal off Iranian underground missile infrastructure rather than vaporize whole cities.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's summary of contemporary Israeli-American military thinking.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that Israel's existing nuclear doctrine is strategic rather than tactical: if Israel's existence is threatened, it would use its arsenal to destroy the world rather than fight a limited nuclear battle.
claims.temporal_scope: General doctrinal model restated in the 2026-03-09 interview.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that under an eschatological reading of the war, Israel does not need to defeat Iran directly; it needs America and Iran to destroy each other so Israel can emerge regionally dominant.
claims.temporal_scope: Strategic model articulated in the interview dated 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang says a limited ground intervention is more plausible than a tactical nuclear strike because it better serves the strategy of luring America into a losing war.
Jiang predicts that Putin will eventually place Iran under a Russian nuclear umbrella, making any nuclear strike on Iran a direct attack on Russia.
Jiang argues that parachuting American troops into central Iran would be pure suicide and politically humiliating because the force would likely be killed or captured.
Jiang says the June twelve-day war unified Iran because Israel burned years of intelligence infrastructure, lost the element of surprise, and provoked a hard Iranian response.
claims.temporal_scope: Retrospective reading of events earlier in 2026.

claims.claim: Jiang describes the January protests as part of a color-revolution playbook involving Mossad-linked embedded agents and says Iranian countermeasures disrupted those networks.
claims.temporal_scope: Claim about protests earlier in 2026 as described on 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang says the Kurds have refused an American offer to join the war and that a serious invasion would therefore require America to enter largely by itself.
claims.temporal_scope: Contemporary war update in the interview dated 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that a workable invasion would require at least half a million troops staged through Pakistan or Iraq, but current drone and missile strikes against GCC infrastructure make such preparation difficult.
Jiang says Israel's main lesson from the twelve-day war was to censor its own public so that footage of damage would not circulate globally.
Jiang argues that despite visible damage and emigration, Israeli society is more resilient and fanatical than outside observers assume because it sees the conflict in end-times terms.
claims.temporal_scope: Contemporary social diagnosis in the 2026-03-09 interview.

claims.claim: Jiang says the truly vulnerable actors are the GCC states because they finance, host, and spatially enable American military operations against Iran.

Jiang argues that Iran's first strategic moves were to de facto close the Strait of Hormuz and hit GCC targets in order to choke the global economy and pressure the American military support structure.

Jiang relays competing explanations for a strike on Saudi oil infrastructure, including his view that Iran would prefer military targets and the suspicion that Mossad-linked false-flag sabotage could be used to widen the war.
Jiang says Israel benefits from dragging Saudi Arabia and other GCC states into the war because that lets Iran weaken future Greater Israel targets in advance.
Jiang argues that the war has shifted from military targets to soft targets that destroy economic life, citing desalination plants as evidence that this is now total war.
claims.temporal_scope: Contemporary escalation diagnosis in the interview dated 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts that within a year or two the end of cheap oil will force many states into de-industrialization and mercantilist rebuilding of self-sufficient supply chains.

Jiang identifies Japan as especially exposed because of its dependence on Hormuz-linked oil and predicts that the wider consequence will be a near-term global development depression and the death of the old trade order.
Jiang predicts that within two to three months public understanding of China and the international order will shift radically.
Jiang says Trump is scheduled for a state visit to Beijing at the end of the month and that this is part of a larger series of meetings pointing toward U.S.-China rapprochement.
claims.temporal_scope: Forthcoming diplomatic timetable described in the 2026-03-09 interview.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that the United States and China are symbiotic beneficiaries of globalization and therefore have strong incentives to strike a grand bargain rather than rush into direct war.
Jiang says China is export-dependent, lacks a sufficient domestic consumer base, and needs energy and food imports, which makes a quick arrangement with America strategically attractive.
claims.temporal_scope: Structural economic diagnosis offered in the interview dated 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts that a Trump-Xi bargain could involve China buying American oil rather than Iranian oil as the Middle East becomes too unstable for existing supply assumptions.
Jiang says Washington presented Iran with three impossible demands: zero uranium enrichment, abandonment of regional proxies, and drastic reduction of ballistic missiles, all without sanction relief.
claims.temporal_scope: Retrospective account of prewar negotiations as described on 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that those impossible demands show the Americans were looking for a pretext to attack Iran rather than a viable peace arrangement.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of prewar diplomacy given on 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang says the Omani foreign minister publicly indicated that Iran had already compromised on enrichment, proxies, and missiles shortly before the attack, which he treats as proof that diplomacy was never the real aim.
claims.temporal_scope: Sequence of events immediately before the attack as narrated on 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that the Americans and Israelis keep their stated strategy vague because their real objective is to destroy Iran as a nation-state, partition it, and rule the fragments indirectly.
claims.temporal_scope: Core war-purpose diagnosis in the interview dated 2026-03-09.

claims.claim: Jiang says there may still be mediation attempts by GCC states or China, but he maintains that the Americans remain committed to Iran's destruction.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang renames the hoped-for end state as Pax Judaica, making Israeli victory sound like a new civilizational order rather than a single battlefield win.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase compresses the interview's entire geopolitical frame into an imperial title that links conquest, finance, technology, and prophecy.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says Israel's nuclear logic is not retaliation but world-ending destruction if its existence is threatened.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Israel is not retaliating, but Israel is just destroying the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the nuclear discussion from technical battlefield speculation into a claim about the absolute structure of Israeli deterrence.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang flips the logic of victory by saying Israel does not need to beat Iran at all; it only needs America and Iran to wreck each other.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Israel just needs America and Iran to destroy each other
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the interview's sharpest strategic reversal, recoding apparent Israeli risk-taking as a trap structure.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang dismisses an airborne insertion into Iran as pure suicide.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase compresses his view that American tactical fantasies collapse when confronted with Iran's scale and cohesion.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says the war has become total war because both sides now have to destroy each other's economies rather than only each other's armies.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: That phrase is the hinge from battlefield commentary into Jiang's much bigger civilizational argument about energy, industry, and world-order collapse.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang declares the old world order of global trade dead in our lifetimes.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the old world order of global trade is dead
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses the packet's economic escalation thesis into a stark terminal verdict rather than a temporary price shock.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang counters the expected U.S.-China war script by calling America and China symbiotic and codependent.
signature_moments.source_phrase: They are symbiotic. They are codependent
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase reframes China from the next battlefield into the indispensable partner inside the same failing global order.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang states the real purpose of the war as bombing Iran back to the Stone Age and breaking it into controllable enclaves.
signature_moments.source_phrase: bomb Iran back to the Stone Age
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the closing diagnosis its hardest edge by moving from negotiation failure to explicit civilizational destruction.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang closes by granting the possibility of peace talks only to snap back to the claim that America is set on destruction.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the Americans are set on the destruction of Iran
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The closing line preserves the interview's governing thesis even after the host asks for an off-ramp.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's name for an Israeli expansionist project stretching from the Nile to the Euphrates and incorporating neighboring states after American decline in the region.
A Jerusalem-centered trading, financial, and technological empire that Jiang presents as the imperial form of a Greater Israel victory.
The transcript's label for Israel's all-or-nothing nuclear doctrine: if the state's existence is threatened, it uses its full strategic arsenal rather than a limited tactical strike.
Jiang's forecast that Russia may extend deterrent protection over Iran so that a nuclear strike there would be treated as an attack on Russia itself.
Jiang's phrase for protest destabilization that combines embedded agents, paid operatives, cyber tools, and foreign intelligence support.
The Gulf monarchies that Jiang treats as the logistical and financial rear of American military power and therefore the most exposed regional weak point.
The maritime chokepoint whose effective closure Jiang treats as a lever for strangling the global economy and exposing energy-dependent states such as Japan.
Jiang's term for the post-cheap-energy world in which states rebuild self-sufficient supply chains and retreat from open global trade.
Jiang's label for a U.S.-China reset in which both powers renegotiate the international order because each still depends on the other's role in globalization.
Jiang's name for Washington's negotiating terms to Iran: zero enrichment, surrender of proxies, and missile rollback without meaningful sanction relief.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates his opening forecast to the present war and then projects its consequences out four to five years into a wider regional transformation.
Jiang distinguishes two recent episodes inside Iran: the June twelve-day war, which exposed Israeli networks, and later January protests, which he folds into a broader color-revolution story.
Jiang explicitly projects the energy and trade consequences forward in two phases: a one-to-two-year de-industrialization turn and a global depression whose first reverberations appear within weeks.
Jiang anchors his China forecast to a specific near-term diplomatic sequence: an end-of-month Trump visit to Beijing and two more planned meetings later in the year.
The packet places the claimed Iranian compromise only a few hours before the Israeli attack, which Jiang uses to argue that war had already been chosen.
This final segment is a closing coda rather than a new scenario: it softens the certainty of immediate escalation only by acknowledging that mediation attempts may occur.

uncertainty_notes.note: Several concrete details here are explicitly framed by Jiang as rumors or public suspicion rather than confirmed facts, especially around alleged explosives at Al-Aqsa.
The transcript renders the doctrine name as 'assumption option'; the intended term may differ, but the underlying all-out nuclear-doctrine argument is clear from context.
The claims about embedded Mossad agents, paid protesters, and Starlink-jamming success are presented assertively by Jiang but are not independently established within the packet itself.
The transcript phrase 'indigenous killing' appears to be ASR corruption; the broader point about Iranian retaliation and national unification is still clear from context.
Jiang marks the drone-strike attribution and the Qatar/Mossad story as controversial or denied in public reporting even while treating false-flag sabotage as plausible.
The transcript phrase 'weapons-grade uranium arrangement' appears to contain ASR corruption, but the intended point about public claims of imminent Iranian breakout is still clear.
